sources.title: Comparative Political Science Literature on PR vs FPTP Outcomes
sources.author: Various

nodes.statement: The 2024 election was the most disproportional in British history with 57.8% of voters unrepresented, Labour winning 411 seats on 33.7% of the vote while Reform UK won 14.3% of votes but only 5 seats and the Greens won 6.7% but only 4 seats

Under proportional representation the ERS projects the 2024 result would have been Labour 228, Conservative 139, Reform 100, Green 71, requiring Labour to form a coalition and negotiate honest compromises with partner parties

Comparative political science research consistently finds PR systems produce higher voter satisfaction, higher turnout, better representation of women and minorities, and policy outcomes closer to median voter preferences
YouGov polling in 2025 shows 49% support PR and 26% favour FPTP with support for FPTP at an all-time low, while four parties gained over 10% of the vote in 2024 for the first time ever

The House of Lords is the second-largest legislative chamber in the world after China's NPC, with the last three PMs creating over 120 peers through PM patronage which the ERS describes as stuffing unlimited new peers on a whim

Scotland has allowed 16-year-olds to vote since 2014 and Austria since 2007, with evidence showing higher turnout among 16-17 year olds than 18-24 year olds through habit formation and no evidence of less informed voting

New Zealand switched from FPTP to MMP in 1996 with government quality maintained or improved, higher Maori representation, and more centrist evidence-based policy outcomes — the most relevant precedent for the UK
FPTP produces better governance than proportional representation systems
nodes.verdict: EVIDENCE FAVOURS PR. PR countries show higher satisfaction, turnout, women's representation, and median-aligned policy. The instability objection is not supported cross-nationally. Germany, Netherlands, Scandinavia, NZ all have stable effective PR governments.

nodes.statement: Proportional representation would change policy outcomes in the UK toward more evidence-based positions
nodes.verdict: YES. Coalition negotiation makes the fiscal conspiracy of silence harder to maintain. Tax locks would be challenged. Environmental policy stronger with Greens holding balance of power. 2024 under PR would have required Labour to negotiate with Lib Dems or Greens, producing policy closer to the evidence base.

nodes.statement: There is sufficient public support for PR to make electoral reform politically achievable
nodes.verdict: YES AND GROWING. 49% support PR vs 26% for FPTP. But the structural trap: the party with power to reform (Labour) benefits most from FPTP. Public opinion has shifted since the 2011 AV referendum which was a failed process not evidence against PR.

nodes.statement: The House of Lords has democratic legitimacy through appointment by elected governments
nodes.verdict: NO DEMOCRATIC JUSTIFICATION. Every other major democracy has elected second chambers or unicameral systems. PM patronage is indefensible. Hereditary peer removal was necessary but insufficient.

nodes.statement: FPTP is the structural cause of the policy failures identified across every other domain because it creates a system that rewards dishonesty and punishes truth-telling through safe seats without accountability and swing seats where populism dominates

Electoral reform is an upstream intervention that changes the structural conditions producing bad policy in every domain, making it the longest dependency chain in the manifesto: electoral structure leads to policy incentives leads to outcomes leads to public service failure

The 2024 result proves the case for reform because the public is already voting as if the UK has PR with four parties over 10% for the first time but the system cannot translate these preferences into representation
Replace FPTP with STV for Westminster elections retaining the constituency link with proportional outcomes in multi-member constituencies, designed by a Citizens' Assembly rather than a referendum
Replace the House of Lords with a smaller elected second chamber of approximately 300 members using PR, transitioning over two parliaments with no PM appointment power
Extend the franchise to 16-year-olds for all UK elections, implement automatic voter registration UK-wide, and abolish or significantly expand voter ID requirements
Cap political donations at £10,000 per year per person, introduce state funding of parties proportional to vote share, and strengthen Electoral Commission independence
Maintain FPTP, voter ID, 18 voting age, and the House of Lords as constituted with no constitutional reform
Extend votes to 16-year-olds, remove hereditary peers, consult on long-term Lords reform, and address voter ID inconsistencies but with no commitment to PR despite 2022 conference vote supporting it
Introduce PR via STV for Westminster and English local government, elect the House of Lords, extend votes to 16, implement automatic voter registration, and cap political donations
Introduce PR for all elections, elect the second chamber, extend votes to 16, implement state funding of parties, and extend the franchise to long-term residents
Hold a referendum on PR, replace the Lords with a smaller elected chamber, introduce direct democracy elements, and impose term limits
